skill.name: workflow-patterns
skill.description: Guides agents through a test-driven development workflow with task selection, phase checkpoints, verification gates, and Git commit practices.
skill.summary: Apply Conductor-style TDD workflows with checkpoints, verification gates, and traceable Git commits.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static command detections are mostly legitimate documentation examples for pytest, ruff, mypy, and Git workflow commands. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential access, malicious networking, obfuscation, or data exfiltration. The skill still warrants a medium warning because it instructs agents to edit project files and run Git commands that can change repository state.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Commands Modify Repository State
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run Git commands such as adding all files, committing changes, adding notes, and reverting commit ranges. These are legitimate workflow steps, but they can alter repository history or include unintended files if run without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly shown in workflow steps and can change local repository state. The context is legitimate development guidance, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Skill Instructs Project File Edits
medium_findings.description: The guide tells agents to update files such as plan.md, tech-stack.md, spec.md, metadata.json, and test files. This is expected for the workflow, but it requires workspace write access and can affect project state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file edit instructions are direct and repeated across task, checkpoint, and deviation workflows. They are appropriate for the skill purpose but should be visible as a filesystem risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography detections occur in prose and Markdown examples, not in cryptographic code or dependency usage. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak algorithms being implemented or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual context review shows these lines are workflow prose, checklist text, or Markdown task status syntax. There is no cryptographic API call or algorithm selection at these locations.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance detections map to normal workflow wording, checklist items, URLs, or example test data. No evidence found of host discovery, port scanning, network probing, or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines describe software verification, user registration examples, or project workflow checks. They do not instruct scanning hosts or collecting system details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes fenced shell examples for pytest, Git, ruff, and mypy. These commands are common in development workflows, but agents should run them only in the intended repository and review Git operations before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell examples are clearly present and operationally relevant. Their behavior is local and expected, with no evidence of malicious command composition.

content.value_statement: Complex coding tasks can lose traceability when tests, commits, and approvals happen out of order. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable TDD workflow with checkpoints and verification gates.
actual_capabilities: Guides task selection from a plan.md file in phase order.
Defines a red, green, refactor cycle for implementation work.
Provides Git commit and git notes patterns for task traceability.
Explains phase checkpoint approval before continuing to later work.
Lists quality gates for tests, coverage, linting, typing, and security.
Documents how to handle deviations, blockers, and checkpoint rejection.
limitations: It is a written workflow guide, not an automation script.
It assumes a repository structure that uses Conductor track plans.
It does not verify test results or coverage by itself.
Git examples can affect local repository state if executed blindly.

use_cases.title: Implement planned tasks with TDD
use_cases.description: Use the lifecycle to select the next task, write failing tests, implement the minimum fix, and refactor safely.

use_cases.title: Review phase completion quality
use_cases.description: Use the checkpoint protocol to confirm tests, coverage, manual verification, and approval before later phases begin.

use_cases.title: Keep AI coding work traceable
use_cases.description: Use commit messages, git notes, and plan.md SHA references to connect implementation work back to tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Start the next task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns skill. Read the current plan.md, identify the next pending task, mark it in progress, and explain the next TDD step.

prompt_templates.title: Apply red green refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns skill to implement this task with TDD. Write failing tests first, make them pass, refactor, and report verification results.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a phase checkpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns skill to prepare a phase checkpoint. List changed files, run the required quality gates, and create manual verification steps for approval.

prompt_templates.title: Recover from a deviation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns skill to handle this implementation deviation. Document the reason, update affected planning files, adjust tasks, and preserve traceability.

output_examples.input: Start Task 2.1 from the plan and follow the workflow.
output: The agent identifies Task 2.1 as the next pending item.
It marks the task in progress before implementation begins.
It writes failing tests, implements the smallest passing change, and records verification results.

output_examples.input: Phase 1 is complete. Prepare the checkpoint.
output: The agent summarizes changed files and test coverage status.
It lists automated checks and manual verification items.
It waits for explicit approval before creating the checkpoint commit.

output_examples.input: The implementation used a library instead of the planned custom code.
output: The agent records the deviation, reason, and impact in the planning materials.
It updates dependency documentation when needed.
It keeps the task traceable through the final commit reference.

best_practices: Run the smallest relevant test set during development, then run full gates at checkpoints.
Review Git commands before execution, especially add-all, commit, notes, and revert operations.
Keep plan.md status, commit messages, and verification notes synchronized.
anti_patterns: Skipping the failing-test step and implementing from assumptions.
Creating checkpoint commits before manual verification is approved.
Using broad Git commands without checking the working tree first.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a workflow guide. An agent may choose to run the documented commands during a coding task.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What workflow does it enforce?
faq.answer: It follows task selection, red-green-refactor implementation, verification, Git commits, and phase checkpoints.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes local shell and Git commands that can change repository state. No malicious intent was found.

faq.question: Does it require a specific project layout?
faq.answer: It works best when the repository has Conductor track files such as plan.md, spec.md, and tech-stack.md.

faq.question: Can it replace human approval?
faq.answer: No. The checkpoint process explicitly waits for user approval before continuing to later phases.
